questions.question: Define a Selection Tree and explain its purpose in data structures. How does it differ from a general binary tree?
keyPoints: Selection Tree is a complete binary tree used for merging k sorted sequences
Leaf nodes represent elements from k sorted lists
Internal nodes store comparison winners (or losers)
Purpose: Efficient k-way merging in O(n log k) time
Differs from general binary trees: Specialized structure with specific ordering property and fixed operations

questions.question: Explain the difference between Winner Trees and Loser Trees. Under what conditions would you prefer one over the other?
keyPoints: Winner Tree: Internal nodes store smaller child (winner)
Loser Tree: Internal nodes store larger child (loser), winner propagates upward
Loser trees require only one comparison per level during update
Winner trees may need two comparisons per level
Prefer loser trees when updates are frequent; winner trees for simpler implementation

questions.question: For k = 11 sorted sequences, determine: (a) Number of dummy leaves needed (b) Total internal nodes after adding dummy leaves (c) Height of the tree
keyPoints: Next power of 2 after 11 is 16
Dummy leaves = 16 - 11 = 5
Total leaves = 16, Internal nodes = 16 - 1 = 15
Tree height = ⌈log₂16⌉ = 4
Formula: height = ⌈log₂(number of leaves)⌉

questions.question: Prove that the time complexity for updating a Selection Tree after outputting one element is O(log k).
keyPoints: A Selection Tree with k leaves is a complete binary tree
Height of complete binary tree with k leaves = ⌈log₂k⌉
Update operation involves re-comparing along path from modified leaf to root
At most one comparison per level on the path
Number of levels = height = ⌈log₂k⌉, therefore O(log k) comparisons

questions.question: Compare Selection Trees with Binary Heaps for k-way merging. Under what circumstances would each be preferred?
keyPoints: Both provide O(log k) update time
Selection Tree construction: O(k), Binary Heap construction: O(k) for k items
Selection Tree has exactly log k comparisons per merge; heap may use more due to heapify
Selection Trees better when merging from same run (next element from same list)
Heaps more versatile for general priority queue operations
Selection Trees have better cache locality due to predictable access pattern

questions.question: Trace the construction of a Winner Tree for merging sequences [2,7,10], [3,8,12], [1,5,9], [6,11]. Show the final tree structure.
keyPoints: Four sequences: 4 leaves needed, already power of 2
Leaf values: 2, 3, 1, 6 (first elements)
Level 2: min(2,1)=1
Root = 1 (smallest element, from sequence 3)
Tree structure: leaves[2,3,1,6], parents[2,1], root[1]

questions.question: Derive the total time complexity for merging k sorted lists with a total of n elements using Selection Trees. Show all steps of your analysis.
keyPoints: Construction Phase: k leaves, k-1 internal nodes, each needs 1 comparison = O(k)
Merging Phase: n output elements, each requires update operation
Update operation: O(log k) comparisons per element
Total time = O(k) + n × O(log k) = O(k + n log k)
When n >> k, simplifies to O(n log k)
Proof by induction or direct analysis of tree height
Space complexity: O(k) for tree nodes plus O(k) for storing current elements

questions.question: Design an algorithm for Loser Tree construction and update operations. Include pseudocode and explain why Loser Trees are more efficient than Winner Trees during updates.
keyPoints: Construction: Initialize tree with +∞, then play tournament bottom-up
Update: New element at leaf competes with stored loser at parent, winner propagates
Pseudocode for update with winner propagation
Why loser trees efficient: At each level, compare new element with stored loser (not both children)
Winner trees need: compare new element with sibling, then compare winner with parent
Loser trees save one comparison per level = log k vs 2×log k comparisons
Proof: Loser tree stores opponent information at each node

questions.question: A hospital needs to merge patient records from 5 departments, each maintaining sorted records by patient ID. Department counts: 120, 85, 200, 150, 45. Calculate using Selection Tree: (a) Number of comparisons for construction (b) Total comparisons for complete merge (c) Height of tree with dummy leaves
keyPoints: k=5, need 8 dummy leaves (next power of 2)
Construction: 8-1 = 7 comparisons
Merge: n × log₂8 = 600 × 3 = 1800 comparisons
Total = 7 + 1800 = 1807 ≈ 1800 (ignoring constants)
Height with 8 leaves = ⌈log₂8⌉ = 3
